sentences.text: My name is Sandor Klein.
It spells in English, S-A-N-D-O-R.
The last name is K-L-E-I-N.
Yeah.
That was my name, always.
19-- November 6th, 1925.
I was born in Hungary.
The town name is Kistarcsa.
You want me to spell it?
That's K-I-S-T-A-R-C-S-A.
That's very close to Budapest, that is the-- of the capital of Hungary.
I really don't remember much because I was 3 and 1/2, close to 4 years old when I left the hometown.
And we moved up very close to Budapest.
The town where we settled down, called Ujpest, U-J-P-E-S-T.
It's about 10 kilometer, which is six miles from the capital city of Budapest.
So I don't have anything especially good to remember of my hometown.
That was a famous place in the later years.
That was a concentration camp, a prison, and-- how can I say it-- where people were killed.
Whom the Germans didn't like, that was a so-called killing place.
I have good and bad memories of the town of Ujpest.
That's where I started to go to the kindergarten.
And the first two years, I went in a public school.
And the second and the third grade, I went to a Jewish congregation school, which was accredited by the State of Hungary.
I had good memories.
I had many of friends.
And we live very close to the synagogue, which was almost opposite from our house.
I grew up there, and I can say I lived in the synagogue and I lived in the house.
My father was not an Orthodox Jew.
He was a Conservative.
But we observed each and every written and unwritten holidays, which belong to our religions.
My father was the president of the congregation.
And I have an older brother who is three years ahead of me.
And my father dream was that one of his sons will be a rabbi.
The other one will be a cantor.
I was scheduled to be a cantor.
I had a good voice.
Later years, I was singing in the choir.
And my brother really followed our father's dream, and he became a rabbi.
He was the Chief Rabbi in Hungary.
This is his part of the life.
So I grew up over there.
And when I reached the age of 11, I was scheduled to go to the high school.
Our schooling system is different than here in the United States.
We had four elementary and four grade in the high school.
And that's where I got the first slam in my face.
I was told I have to go to the number-two high school because Jews was not allowed to go to the number-one school.
That was in 19-- [SIGHS] --'30-- --'34.
That-- we already had the so-called Jewish Law in-- in Hungary.
A couple of years before I reach this we had the Prime Minister, who was a bloody man against the Jews.
He wanted to kill all the Jews since 1919, when a-- a Regent of the country-- his name was Miklos Horthy.
He was the Regent.
And he came in, and he started-- right after the first war ended, he started killing the Jews.
Because he said that all the Jews, you are communist.
And that's went on, and this continued through the years.
Gombos ?] [INAUDIBLE] was the Interior Minister who was handling the case of the Jews.
And since I remember back, let's call it from 1936 till 1939, they came out with, one after the other, the laws and the construction of the Jewish people.
My father name was in Hungarian, Klein, Jeno.
It's spelled J-E-N-O with an umlaut on the top.
My mother name was Serena Reich, R-E-I-C-H.
My father was a shoemaker.
We belonged to the so-called lower-middle class.
We were not very poor, but what we didn't reach the level where you can say we were the upper-middle class.
He was working six days a week, except Saturday because that was observed in our house.
And my mother had a little store out in the marketplace.
She opened the store three times a week.
The other days, except Saturday, she was working at home, cooking, cleaning, and raising both of us children.
We lived very, very quietly, harmonious life in the house.
My brother was the older, and my feeling--
His name is Tibor, T-I-B-O-R.
And his Hebrew name was Joel.
That is the name he is using nowadays.
He was the so-called [GERMAN] of the family.
Because my father wanted him to be the rabbit, and my mother liked this.
I never object at this because I was busy with my friends, going out.
I didn't follow my brother's steps.
I have to tell you, I didn't care too much for the religion.
But I had to do it, so I did it.
I was a good kid.
I was, every day, in the synagogue.
Before the synagogue, we were in the playground.
But when the time came, I had to stop.
My father said go to the synagogue.
Come home, you can play again.
So that was our life.
Going back to the school when I got the slam on my face, I went to the number two high school.
And really, all my friend who were-- most of them were Jewish, we went together to this school.
There was no problem in this school.
The school where my brother went, he went to the-- I don't know how you call this, English.
We called it gymnasium.
That was a so-called higher-class high school.
He went there, and they had disturbance against the Jewish people.
They had an organization which I cannot compare here.
In the school, they had different groups.
We called them the Turul, T-U-R-U-L.
That was a group.
And their main goal was putting out the Jewish student from the school.
He fought against it.
He was an excellent student.
And he finished his schooling.
Then later years, he went to the school where they-- the theology, where the-- the young men studied to be rabbis.
That was his goal, and he went through with this.
I didn't have, really, much else in my life, playing, going to school, studying, and going to the synagogue.
I was a soccer player.
Later years, when I could reach through the group age, I was playing soccer until I was taken out from the team because I was Jewish. 1939, I finished high school.
And after graduation, I applied to a school where the textile engineers were studying.
I went for the aptitude test, written test, verbal test.
I went through both of them with so-called high color.
And I was waiting, that next September I can go to the school.
I got a letter, and they said that you are not accepted to school.
That was the first time in my life I was crying, that how come I was not accepted?
Then we went back to the school with my mother.
And then they told me that the newest law against the Jew does not let me go in the school.
First, we had-- I mentioned the name of the-- of the Interior Minister who brought out the Jewish-- against the Jewish laws.
First, it was the so-called numerous clauses.
This is a name-- it's a Latin name.
It said that only a certain percentage of the student can be from the Jewish faith.
That was the reason my brother could get into the university.
Because he was an excellent student, and he was within the percentage.
When my time came in late 1939, they came out with the numerus nullus, which means no Jews allowed to the school system.
So that, I was hurt very, very much.
But I told my parents that I don't want to be a cantor.
I don't feel like doing this kind of thing.
Let me go and learn the trade.
And I went to a factory which was very close to our house in the city of Ujpest.
And I signed up for-- recall the name, I think-- apprenticed.
I signed up for a three-year, standard time to study the trade, learned the trade, and after, if I go and pass the test, I will be on my own.
I worked over there for two years.
That was 1940, February, till 1942.
February, and my boss told me that he thinks I am ready for the test, even though I have another year to go.
I pass the test, and I worked.
And the money I earned, I helped our family to going through the harder time, which were coming.
That was-- that was my part until I was entered in the armed services in 1944, May.
Yes.
Yes.
Friday noon, everything came to a standstill in our house.
Started with the personal cleaning.
My mother covered the table with the white tablecloth.
The two candle holder went on the middle of the table.
The big challah, which my mother baked Thursday afternoon, was ready, put on the table, and covered with the handmade cover.
There was the cup, the Kiddish cup for the wine, a prayer book prepared for my father, and everything turned to silent.
Then, we went to the synagogue Friday afternoon, three of us.
My mother stayed home.
And when we came home, the candle was already lit.
Then my father said the prayer after the challah, cut it up, and we all sat down and we ate.
That was the really only festive meal we had all together.
Every single Friday afternoon and Friday night, and every single holiday night, it was like this.
After the dinner, we talked about what happened all week long.
Then my father say the prayer.
When the dinner was over, my mother took the dishes away.
And as far as I can remember back, my father put his head on his arm and slept at the table.
That was our-- our Shabbat night at home.
Next morning, we got up.
We got breakfast, and we went to the synagogue.
We came home from there around noontime.
And after the big lunch, we all had rest.
We all went to our rooms and we rested for a while.
This continued later afternoon.
We went back to the synagogue again.
And then came Saturday night.
We did the Havdalah service at home.
We said goodbye to the sabbath and started the week over and over, and over again.
My father started early in the morning, Sunday morning, and the week started.
We kids had the day off Sunday.
We played.
We [? prayed-- ?] we prepared for the schools, and then Monday, we started our week also.
The favorite holiday was really the-- the Sukkot, which is coming after the High Holidays.
That was not a strict holiday.
We were not supposed to be in the synagogue all day long.
We were free to roam.
We had lots and lots of Jewish kids in the congregation.
There was-- the city of Ujpest had over 100,000 Jews.
We had three synagogues, two big one, one smaller one.
Ours was the biggest one.
That was a Conservative synagogue.
Temple was always full, Friday night, Saturday morning, and all the holidays.
And we kids had to be in only at the beginning and the end.
In between, we could-- we could go out.
And we'd play.
And I like the Sukkot.
We had a huge Sukkot out there in the yard of the synagogue.
And we all spent time over there.
And after the service was over, our rabbi-- whom we all liked very much.
He was a young rabbi-- he came out, and he always-- not played with us.
But he was part of the so-called gang.
That was the liked one.
I liked all the holidays.
And I observed them, and I still do.
Every single holiday has a different memory I can remember the Shavuot, which is after-- seven weeks after Passover.
Our synagogue was beautiful, decorated with the leaves and flowers and fresh flower.
And we liked the second day of this.
Because that's where the girls had their confirmation in the afternoon.
And we boys were all over there, looking for the girls.
And after the service was over we were allowed to go with the girls and talk to them or whatever we wanted to do with the girls.
The High Holidays was somber.
I was told what is the meaning of the High Holidays.
We lived according to these.
And at that time, I took everything the way it was said.
I was told you do this.
You don't do this.
And I lived according to this.
I try not to cheat.
That was our life, and that was our father's idea, that we live according to the written words.
I-- I have memory only of my-- my two grandmothers.
The grandfathers, one of them died.
My father's father died before I was born.
The other one was-- died exactly one month after I was born.
So I never had a chance-- chance, even, to see them.
My mother's grandmother lived in the capital city of Hungary, in Budapest.
And that was about 10 kilometer from us.
I didn't have too much chance to go and see them.
My father's mother, my grandmother, and my father's sister lived in the same town, in Ujpest, where we were-- I had the chance to see her.
But I don't remember that our family, further than four of us, was close enough.
So I just knew this is my grandmother.
I ran into her house.
Let's say, once a week I said hi to her.
And she lived with her daughter, who was my father's sister.
She was a-- a widow.
Her husband died, and she had two children.
The two children, somehow, were not too close to me.
They were younger than me and we didn't have too much connection to each other.
So-- and my-- my father's mother went to Auschwitz, which I will talk about it later.
And she died over there.
And my mother's mother survived the Holocaust in Budapest, in the ghetto.
And she died after-- after the-- years after the-- the war was over.
So that's all my memory about my grandparents.
Yes, we had radio.
Until we could have the radio in the house-- with one of the law against the Jews, where that we were not allowed to listen or use a radio in the house, we had to all go.
Every Jew had to go to the city hall with the radio, or if they had any other-- there was no television in that time.
We had to go and they so-called stamped the radio, which means we can't-- we are not able to plug it into any outlet.
So we had no real contact with the outside world, what's happening outside of our house.
I had a guitar, a musical instrument myself.
But somehow, I was so busy I never had the chance really, to-- to learn how to play the guitar correctly.
Yes.
I had one.
I had-- they lived in the same apartment building where we were.
We were very, very close.
It's funny to say it.
But even the later years, when the Hungarian fascist came to rule in the town, he joined them.
And he even anticipated in killing the Jews.
His name was Bela Kiss, K-I-S-S.
The first name was B-E-L-A.
And after the war, believe it or not, when I returned to Budapest, I never went back to live in the-- the town.
We went out at the first time the memorial wall was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --dedicated.
We went out too.
We passed my father and my brother and myself.
And I was looking for this man.
And he told me what he-- what did he do in the war.
And he even asked me, will you report me?
Mhm.
Will you put me in the concentration camp for this?
I said no.
You were my best friend.
We grew up together.
We have to forget the bad thing.
We just remember the good one.
And I had contact with him until we came out to the United States.
He was my really-- the best friend.
I was-- I had good friends.
They were all Jewish.
But he was the only friend to my-- so he was my best friend.
We really lived together.
Every problem, every happiness, every bad thing, every-- the good thing what we did, we did together.
Good.
It was a good thing.
The city-- the people of the city lived in harmony.
I don't remember any disturbance against the Jews.
We all went through hard times.
Jews were not allowed to have their own shop.
My mother were not allowed to keep-- here I'm talking about the years after 1940.
My father worked at home.
He didn't have a shop.
So he was not disturbed by this law.
He could work at home.
He had hard time to get parts, what he needed to making the shoes.
But somehow, he managed it.
Even the-- the non-Jewish friend of his helped him.

Until everything was stopped by the government, which came in 19-- 1943, when the-- the Jewish laws, the so-called Jewish laws, were so strict that they were told they will get into trouble if they will have the contact with the Jewish people.
So that's when they started to apart from us, and we really were-- were say, frozen.
We were on our own.
And my father was inducted in the armed services first time, 1940.
And then he came home after six months because one of the law let him come home because he had two children.
He was called back again, 1942.
And since he was a shoemaker, he didn't serve with weapon.
He was the shoemaker in the armed services.
And he was there until 1944, the time I was inducted into the services.
And I saw my father after this, 1945, August, end of August.
Didn't know where he was.
I knew where he went, to the service.
But further, we didn't know which city or what group he was.
And two years after I was through with my learning of the trade, I was working in a-- a small factory, which owned by the Jewish man.
He was very nice to me.
And I was working over there in 1944, May.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's when I was inducted into the service.
I have to jump back for about a good month. 1944, March 19.
The German army occupied Hungary.
And they occupied the capital city of Budapest.
I was in a theater, in a matinee show in the afternoon.
And the president of the theater came out and says that the program which was scheduled to go on now, for a reason which we are not telling you people, cannot be shown.
And we will play something else.
So we had a different show.
And the intermission we went out to the balcony and looked on the city.
And we saw the motorcycle, the side-sitter motorcycles full of German SS soldiers.
That's when the occupation started.
We had the hard-- hard time to get home from the theater after.
Because they started to ask identification for each and every person in the street.
They already had-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --they already had a list of the people who wanted to get together.
So we had to show the identification book, which we all had.
And finally, we arrive home.
We sat down.
And we started to talk, what's next?
We didn't have the radio.
There was no television.
We just heard what we heard on the streets.
And then they came to our town also.
Next morning, they started to get the signs on the street walls, everywhere.
The Jews are not allowed to leave their house after six o'clock at night.
You are not allowed to be on the street before seven o'clock in the morning.
And it came, the law, that we all had to put on a 10-centimeter-big-- which is about four inches-- canary-yellow-colored star.
We all had to make it somehow, somewhere, some fabric.
And we had to put on our clothes.
I tell you the truth, I didn't do it.
Because I was not going through-- I was not going to go to work with the star on my-- and somehow, I just felt that why should I be different today than I was yesterday?
So I was rebelling against the thing.
Which later, I was sorry for it.
But I had to put it on later on.
And my boss was called into the armed services, and he closed down the factory.
So that's when the bad things started.
Most of the factories in the town are owned by Jewish people.
We had big factories.
The town was an industrial town, call it city.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had leather factories, shoe factories, textile factories, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] carpenters factory.
They made furnitures over there.
